metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Spoken Language 10 develops students' oral communication skills through a range of spoken forms including oral storytelling, debate, spoken word and slam poetry, recitation, presentations, and podcast/radio production. Students explore how voice is a powerful creative and persuasive tool, and how First Peoples oral traditions model the depth and richness of spoken language. The course builds confidence, clarity, and intentionality in public speaking and oral performance.
metadata.general_description: A focused study of spoken language and oral communication, developing confidence and skill in oral storytelling, debate, spoken word, presentation, and performance through understanding of voice as a powerful creative tool.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: Oral stories, performances, and speeches are as rich and complex as written texts — and can communicate meaning that written language sometimes cannot.

big_ideas.statement: People understand text differently depending on their world views and perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Listeners bring their own identities and experiences to what they hear — meaning is co-created between speaker and audience.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every oral performance reflects its cultural and historical context — from the oral traditions of First Peoples to the slam poetry of contemporary urban culture.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: Spoken language is one of the most powerful tools humans have — a speech, a story, or a poem can move people to tears, laughter, or action.
The human voice — its tone, pace, rhythm, and inflection — carries meaning beyond words. Skilled speakers use their voice as a creative instrument.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Recognize and appreciate the role of story, narrative, and oral tradition in expressing First Peoples perspectives, values, beliefs, and points of view.
i_can_statements: I can explain the role of oral tradition in First Peoples cultures as a way of transmitting history, law, and values.
I can listen to a First Peoples oral performance with respectful attention and cultural awareness.
I can identify specific oral techniques in a First Peoples performance (e.g., repetition, rhythm, narrative structure).

competencies.raw: Recognize and appreciate the diversity among First Peoples cultures; access information from diverse sources.
i_can_statements: I can identify specific First Nations communities whose oral traditions I am studying.
I can access oral histories, recordings, and performances from diverse First Peoples communities.
I can explain why it's important to recognize diversity within First Peoples cultures, including their oral traditions.

competencies.raw: Apply appropriate strategies to comprehend written, oral, visual, and multimodal texts; identify bias, contradictions, and distortions.
i_can_statements: I can listen critically to a speech, podcast, or oral performance and analyze its techniques and purpose.
I can identify rhetorical techniques (pathos, ethos, logos) in spoken texts.
I can recognize bias, distortion, or manipulative techniques in spoken media.

competencies.raw: Explore how language constructs personal and cultural identities; construct meaningful personal connections.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how a speaker's voice and language reflect their cultural identity.
I can connect a spoken text to my own experiences and use that connection to deepen my understanding.
I can reflect on how my own voice and communication style is shaped by my background and experiences.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Respectfully exchange ideas and viewpoints from diverse perspectives to build shared understanding.
i_can_statements: I can engage in structured discussions and debates, listening carefully and responding thoughtfully.
I can build on others' contributions in a collaborative oral task.
I can disagree respectfully and explain my reasoning clearly.

competencies.raw: Assess and refine texts to improve clarity and impact; demonstrate speaking and listening skills in formal and informal contexts.
i_can_statements: I can rehearse and refine an oral presentation for clarity, expression, and impact.
I can adapt my speaking style to different contexts (informal discussion, formal debate, performance).
I can self-assess my speaking performance and set specific goals for improvement.

competencies.raw: Explore appropriate spoken language formats for intended purposes; express and support opinions with evidence.
i_can_statements: I can choose and justify the right spoken form for my purpose (debate, storytelling, spoken word, presentation).
I can deliver an argument orally with clear reasoning and supporting evidence.
I can use rhetorical techniques intentionally in my spoken performances.

competencies.raw: Transform ideas and information to create original texts; use acknowledgements and citations.
i_can_statements: I can create original spoken texts — stories, poems, speeches, podcasts — that reflect my own voice and perspective.
I can transform written material into an engaging oral presentation.
I can acknowledge sources and oral traditions I've drawn on in my performances.

areas.name: Spoken Text Forms and Genres
areas.raw: Spoken word/slam poetry, recitation, oral storytelling, readers' theatre, debate, radio/podcasts/social media, presentations, public service announcements (PSAs).
topics: Spoken word and slam poetry: personal, political, and artistic performance
Oral storytelling: traditional and contemporary forms
Recitation: memorization and expressive delivery of texts
Readers' theatre: collaborative oral interpretation
Formal debate: structure, argument, rebuttal
Podcasts and radio drama: audio storytelling and journalism
Presentations and public speaking: informational and persuasive
Public Service Announcements (PSAs): short persuasive oral/multimodal texts
First Peoples oral texts and protocols

areas.name: Text Features and Structures
areas.raw: Oral text features and structures; narrative structures found in First Peoples texts; First Peoples oral traditions; protocols related to ownership.
topics: Features of oral texts: rhythm, repetition, call-and-response, silence, gesture
Structure of debate: proposition, argument, rebuttal, conclusion
Structure of oral narrative: setup, rising action, climax, resolution
First Peoples oral narrative structures: circular, iterative, cyclical
Protocols for engaging with and sharing First Peoples oral texts

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies, oral language strategies, metacognitive strategies, writing processes and presentation techniques.
topics: Listening strategies: active listening, noting key points, questioning, paraphrasing
Speaking strategies: projection, pacing, pausing, eye contact, gesture
Rehearsal strategies: recording, self-evaluation, peer feedback
Writing to speak: scripting, cue cards, memorization
Metacognitive reflection on speaking and listening performance

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Features of oral language; elements of style; rhetorical devices and persuasive techniques; usage and conventions; literary elements and devices; citation techniques.
topics: Voice: tone, volume, pace, inflection, emphasis
Rhetorical devices: parallelism, repetition, anaphora, tricolon, rhetorical questions
Persuasive techniques: ethos, pathos, logos
Figurative language in oral performance: metaphor, imagery, personification
Register and formality: adapting language to context and audience
Citation and acknowledgement in oral contexts
